Base-$b$ ramp digits with order-$4$ cycle and exact $p$-adic/$q$-adic divisibility; sum of valid shifts modulo $1000$

Let $b=26412$ and $L=35$. For each integer $t$ with $0\le t\le 12004$, define
$N_t=\sum_{i=0}^{34} (t+i)b^i$.
Since $t+34\le 12038<b$, the numbers $t,t+1,\dots,t+34$ are valid base-$b$ digits, so $N_t$ is the base-$b$ integer whose least significant digit is $t$ and whose $i$th digit is $t+i$.

For a prime $r$ and nonzero integer $X$, let $v_r(X)$ be the largest integer $e\ge 0$ such that $r^e\mid X$.

Let $S=\{t\in\{0,1,\dots,12004\}: v_7(N_t)=4\ \text{and}\ v_5(N_t)=1\}$ and let $T=\sum_{t\in S} t$.

Determine the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that $N\equiv T\pmod{1000}$.

Solution.
Step 1 (positional_value_expansion).
Define $A=\sum_{i=0}^{34} b^i$ and $B=\sum_{i=0}^{34} i\,b^i$.
Then
$N_t=\sum_{i=0}^{34}(t+i)b^i=t\sum_{i=0}^{34} b^i+\sum_{i=0}^{34} i\,b^i=tA+B$.

Step 2 (divisibility_via_digits and LTE).
We have $b-1=26411=7^4\cdot 11$, so $7^4\parallel(b-1)$ and $b\equiv 1\pmod{7^4}$.
Hence $b^i\equiv 1\pmod{7^4}$ for all $i$, so
$A=\sum_{i=0}^{34} b^i\equiv \sum_{i=0}^{34}1=35\pmod{7^4}$,
$B=\sum_{i=0}^{34} i\,b^i\equiv \sum_{i=0}^{34} i=\frac{34\cdot 35}{2}=595\pmod{7^4}$,
and therefore
$N_t=tA+B\equiv 35t+595=35(t+17)\pmod{7^4}$.

Lemma 1.
Let $p$ be an odd prime and suppose $p\mid(b-1)$. For every integer $L\ge 1$,
$v_p(b^L-1)=v_p(b-1)+v_p(L)$.
In particular, for $p=7$ and $L=35$ we have $v_7(A)=1$.

Proof.
By LTE, since $p$ is odd and $p\mid(b-1)$ and $p\nmid b$, we have $v_p(b^L-1)=v_p(b-1)+v_p(L)$.
Since $A=(b^L-1)/(b-1)$, this gives $v_p(A)=v_p(L)$, so for $p=7$ and $L=35$ we get $v_7(A)=v_7(35)=1$.

Step 3 (linear_congruence_solving for the $7$-part).
From Step 2, $7^4\mid N_t$ is equivalent to $7^4\mid 35(t+17)$.
Since $35=7\cdot 5$ and $\gcd(5,7)=1$, we have
$7^4\mid 35(t+17)\iff 7^3\mid (t+17)$.
Thus
$7^4\mid N_t\iff t\equiv -17\pmod{343}$.
Equivalently, $t=-17+343s$ for some integer $s$.

Step 4 (cycle_decomposition_mod for the $5$-part).
We have $b\equiv 26412\equiv 2\pmod{5}$, hence $b^2\equiv 4\equiv -1\pmod{5}$ and $b^4\equiv 1\pmod{5}$.
Therefore $1+b+b^2+b^3=(1+b)(1+b^2)\equiv 0\pmod{5}$, so powers of $b$ have period $4$ modulo $5$.

Since $35=4\cdot 8+3$, eight full blocks of length $4$ contribute $0$ to $\sum_{i=0}^{34} b^i$ modulo $5$, leaving
$A=\sum_{i=0}^{34} b^i\equiv 1+b+b^2\pmod{5}$.
Using $b^2\equiv -1\pmod{5}$ gives $A\equiv b\equiv 2\pmod{5}$.

To compute $B=\sum_{i=0}^{34} i\,b^i$ modulo $5$, write $i=4j+r$ with $r\in\{0,1,2,3\}$.
For $r=0,1,2$ we have $j=0,\dots,8$, and for $r=3$ we have $j=0,\dots,7$.
Using $b^{4j}\equiv 1$, $b^{4j+1}\equiv b$, $b^{4j+2}\equiv b^2$, $b^{4j+3}\equiv b^3$ modulo $5$, we get
$B\equiv \sum_{j=0}^8 (4j)\cdot 1+b\sum_{j=0}^8 (4j+1)+b^2\sum_{j=0}^8 (4j+2)+b^3\sum_{j=0}^7 (4j+3)\pmod{5}$.
Compute the integer sums:
$\sum_{j=0}^8 4j=144\equiv 4\pmod{5}$,
$\sum_{j=0}^8 (4j+1)=153\equiv 3\pmod{5}$,
$\sum_{j=0}^8 (4j+2)=162\equiv 2\pmod{5}$,
$\sum_{j=0}^7 (4j+3)=136\equiv 1\pmod{5}$.
Also $b\equiv 2$, $b^2\equiv 4$, $b^3\equiv 3$ modulo $5$, so
$B\equiv 4+2\cdot 3+4\cdot 2+3\cdot 1\equiv 1\pmod{5}$.

Thus $N_t=tA+B\equiv 2t+1\pmod{5}$, and
$5\mid N_t\iff 2t+1\equiv 0\pmod{5}\iff t\equiv 2\pmod{5}$.

Step 5 (Chinese_remainder_theorem and candidate enumeration).
Combine Step 3 and Step 4:
$t\equiv -17\pmod{343}$ and $t\equiv 2\pmod{5}$.
Write $t=-17+343s$ and reduce modulo $5$ to get $-17+343s\equiv 3+3s\pmod{5}$, so $3+3s\equiv 2\pmod{5}$ and hence $s\equiv 3\pmod{5}$.
Thus $s=3+5j$ and
$t=-17+343(3+5j)=1012+1715j$.

Since $1715=343\cdot 5$ and $12005=1715\cdot 7$, the values $j=0,1,\dots,6$ give exactly all $t\in\{0,1,\dots,12004\}$ satisfying $7^4\mid N_t$ and $5\mid N_t$:
$t_0=1012$, $t_1=2727$, $t_2=4442$, $t_3=6157$, $t_4=7872$, $t_5=9587$, $t_6=11302$.

Step 6 (prime_power_lifting_step for the exact $7$-adic valuation).
Write $b=1+7^4u$ with $u=11$.
For every $i\ge 0$, the binomial expansion gives
$b^i=(1+7^4u)^i\equiv 1+i\cdot 7^4u\pmod{7^5}$.

Substitute into $N_t=\sum_{i=0}^{34}(t+i)b^i$ to get
$N_t\equiv \sum_{i=0}^{34}(t+i)+7^4u\sum_{i=0}^{34} i(t+i)\pmod{7^5}$.

Lemma 2.
For $L=35$, the sum $\sum_{i=0}^{34} i(t+i)$ is divisible by $7$ for every integer $t$.

Proof.
We have $\sum_{i=0}^{34} i=\frac{34\cdot 35}{2}=595=7\cdot 85$ and
$\sum_{i=0}^{34} i^2=\frac{34\cdot 35\cdot 69}{6}=13685=7\cdot 1955$.
Then $\sum_{i=0}^{34} i(t+i)=t\sum_{i=0}^{34} i+\sum_{i=0}^{34} i^2$ is divisible by $7$.

By Lemma 2, the term $7^4u\sum i(t+i)$ is divisible by $7^5$, so
$N_t\equiv \sum_{i=0}^{34}(t+i)=35t+595=35(t+17)\pmod{7^5}$.
Therefore,
$7^5\mid N_t\iff 7^5\mid 35(t+17)\iff 7^4\mid (t+17)\iff t\equiv -17\pmod{2401}$.

Among the candidates $t=-17+343s$ with $s=3+5j$, we have $t\equiv -17\pmod{2401}$ if and only if $2401\mid 343s$, which is equivalent to $7\mid s$.
Since $s=3+5j$, the congruence $3+5j\equiv 0\pmod{7}$ has the unique solution $j\equiv 5\pmod{7}$, so for $j\in\{0,1,\dots,6\}$ the only solution is $j=5$.
Thus exactly one candidate has $7^5\mid N_t$, namely $t_5=9587$, and it must be excluded to achieve $v_7(N_t)=4$.

Step 7 (prime_power_lifting_step for the exact $5$-adic valuation).
We decide when $25\mid N_t$.

Lemma 3.
We have $b\equiv 12\pmod{25}$ and $b^4\equiv 11\pmod{25}$, so $b^4=1+5w$ with $5w\equiv 10\pmod{25}$.
In particular $w\equiv 2\pmod{5}$, so $w\not\equiv 0\pmod{5}$.
For every $j\ge 0$, $(b^4)^j\equiv 1+5wj\pmod{25}$, and for $i=4j+r$ with $r\in\{0,1,2,3\}$ we have
$b^i\equiv b^r(1+5wj)\pmod{25}$.

Proof.
Since $(5w)^2$ is divisible by $25$, the binomial theorem gives $(1+5w)^j\equiv 1+5wj\pmod{25}$.
Multiplying by $b^r$ gives the claimed formula for $b^{4j+r}$.

Using Lemma 3 and $5w\equiv 10\pmod{25}$, we have $(b^4)^j\equiv 1+10j\pmod{25}$.
Also $b\equiv 12$, $b^2\equiv 19$, $b^3\equiv 3$ modulo $25$, so
$b^{4j}\equiv 1+10j$,
$b^{4j+1}\equiv 12(1+10j)\equiv 12+20j$,
$b^{4j+2}\equiv 19(1+10j)\equiv 19+15j$,
$b^{4j+3}\equiv 3(1+10j)\equiv 3+5j$ modulo $25$.

Compute $A=\sum_{i=0}^{34} b^i$ modulo $25$ by splitting into these residue classes.
For $r=0,1,2$ we sum over $j=0,\dots,8$, and for $r=3$ we sum over $j=0,\dots,7$.
Using $\sum_{j=0}^8 j=36$ and $\sum_{j=0}^7 j=28$, we get
$\sum_{j=0}^8 b^{4j}\equiv \sum_{j=0}^8 (1+10j)=9+10\cdot 36\equiv 19\pmod{25}$,
$\sum_{j=0}^8 b^{4j+1}\equiv \sum_{j=0}^8 (12+20j)=9\cdot 12+20\cdot 36\equiv 3\pmod{25}$,
$\sum_{j=0}^8 b^{4j+2}\equiv \sum_{j=0}^8 (19+15j)=9\cdot 19+15\cdot 36\equiv 11\pmod{25}$,
$\sum_{j=0}^7 b^{4j+3}\equiv \sum_{j=0}^7 (3+5j)=8\cdot 3+5\cdot 28\equiv 14\pmod{25}$,
so $A\equiv 19+3+11+14\equiv 22\pmod{25}$.

Now compute $B=\sum_{i=0}^{34} i\,b^i$ modulo $25$ using the same split:
$B=\sum_{j=0}^8 (4j)b^{4j}+\sum_{j=0}^8 (4j+1)b^{4j+1}+\sum_{j=0}^8 (4j+2)b^{4j+2}+\sum_{j=0}^7 (4j+3)b^{4j+3}$.

Using the congruences for $b^{4j+r}$, we expand each summand:
$(4j)b^{4j}\equiv (4j)(1+10j)=4j+40j^2$,
$(4j+1)b^{4j+1}\equiv (4j+1)(12+20j)=80j^2+68j+12$,
$(4j+2)b^{4j+2}\equiv (4j+2)(19+15j)=60j^2+106j+38$,
$(4j+3)b^{4j+3}\equiv (4j+3)(3+5j)=20j^2+27j+9$ modulo $25$.

Use $\sum_{j=0}^8 j=36$, $\sum_{j=0}^8 j^2=204$, $\sum_{j=0}^7 j=28$, $\sum_{j=0}^7 j^2=140$ to sum:
$\sum_{j=0}^8 (4j+40j^2)=4\cdot 36+40\cdot 204=8304\equiv 4\pmod{25}$,
$\sum_{j=0}^8 (80j^2+68j+12)=80\cdot 204+68\cdot 36+12\cdot 9=18876\equiv 1\pmod{25}$,
$\sum_{j=0}^8 (60j^2+106j+38)=60\cdot 204+106\cdot 36+38\cdot 9=16398\equiv 23\pmod{25}$,
$\sum_{j=0}^7 (20j^2+27j+9)=20\cdot 140+27\cdot 28+9\cdot 8=3628\equiv 3\pmod{25}$.
Therefore $B\equiv 4+1+23+3\equiv 6\pmod{25}$.

Thus $N_t=tA+B\equiv 22t+6\pmod{25}$, and since $\gcd(22,25)=1$,
$25\mid N_t\iff 22t+6\equiv 0\pmod{25}$.
Solve $22t\equiv 19\pmod{25}$.
Since $22\equiv -3\pmod{25}$ and $(-3)^{-1}\equiv 8\pmod{25}$, we get $t\equiv 19\cdot 8\equiv 2\pmod{25}$.

Among the candidates $t_j=1012+1715j$, reduce modulo $25$ to get $t_j\equiv 12+15j\pmod{25}$.
Thus $t_j\equiv 2\pmod{25}$ is equivalent to $15j\equiv 15\pmod{25}$, which reduces to $j\equiv 1\pmod{5}$.
For $j\in\{0,1,\dots,6\}$ this gives $j=1$ and $j=6$, so the $25$-divisible candidates are $t_1=2727$ and $t_6=11302$.
Excluding them enforces $v_5(N_t)=1$.

Step 8 (residue_class_summation and normalization).
From Step 5 there are $7$ candidates with $7^4\mid N_t$ and $5\mid N_t$.
Step 6 excludes exactly one of them for having $7^5\mid N_t$, and Step 7 excludes exactly two of them for having $25\mid N_t$.
The remaining values are $t_0=1012$, $t_2=4442$, $t_3=6157$, $t_4=7872$, and these are exactly the elements of $S$.

Hence $T=1012+4442+6157+7872=19483$, so $T\equiv 483\pmod{1000}$.
The unique $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ with $N\equiv T\pmod{1000}$ is $N=483$.

Answer: 483

3. Uniqueness Test
Each $t\in\{0,1,\dots,12004\}$ defines a unique integer $N_t$, so $S$ is well-defined.

Step 2 and Step 3 show $7^4\mid N_t$ holds for exactly one residue class $t\equiv -17\pmod{343}$.
Step 4 shows $5\mid N_t$ holds for exactly one residue class $t\equiv 2\pmod{5}$.
Since $\gcd(343,5)=1$, CRT gives exactly one residue class modulo $1715$ satisfying both, so in $\{0,1,\dots,12004\}$ there are exactly $12005/1715=7$ candidates, the arithmetic progression $t_j=1012+1715j$ for $j=0,\dots,6$.

Step 6 gives the exact criterion $7^5\mid N_t\iff t\equiv -17\pmod{2401}$.
Inside the progression $t=-17+343s$ with $s=3+5j$, this is equivalent to $7\mid s$, which holds for exactly one $j\in\{0,\dots,6\}$.
Step 7 gives the exact criterion $25\mid N_t\iff t\equiv 2\pmod{25}$.
Inside the progression $t_j\equiv 12+15j\pmod{25}$, this holds for exactly two $j\in\{0,\dots,6\}$.
These three excluded indices are distinct, so exactly $7-3=4$ values of $t$ satisfy simultaneously $v_7(N_t)=4$ and $v_5(N_t)=1$.
Therefore the set $S$ is unique, its sum $T$ is unique, and the normalized residue $N\equiv T\pmod{1000}$ in $\{0,\dots,999\}$ is uniquely forced to be $483$.

483
